cataloging_subject.label: English Gilds
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Joshua Toulmin Smith and Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1870 edited documentary compilation, including Lucy's introduction and glossary, Lujo Brentano's independent preliminary essay, three main documentary parts, two appendices, and an index, as transmitted through a corrected 1892 plate reprint and a 1963 EETS reprint to the U-M encoding. The historical documents are many distinct works and records with many creators and witnesses; the cataloging subject is not one homogeneous medieval work, one physical manuscript, the whole TNA C 47/38–46 series, the printed book as an artifact, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML. The XML represents the title page through Appendix II but omits the 1870 glossarial index and general index on pp. 463–483 and apparently omits the reference-update leaf promised in the retained August 1892 notice.

catalog_summary.title: English Gilds
catalog_summary.author: Named and anonymous medieval and early-modern documentary creators; compiled and edited by Joshua Toulmin Smith and Lucy Toulmin Smith
catalog_summary.editor: Joshua Toulmin Smith; completed by Lucy Toulmin Smith
catalog_summary.date: Documentary sources chiefly fourteenth–fifteenth centuries, with later records and copies; compilation first published 1870 and corrected 1892
catalog_summary.region: England, especially London, Norfolk, Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, and Tettenhall, with records from many additional towns

titles.value: English Gilds: The Original Ordinances of More than One Hundred Early English Gilds, Together with þe Olde Vsages of þe Cite of Wynchestre; the Ordinances of Worcester; the Office of the Mayor of Bristol; and the Costomary of the Manor of Tettenhall-Regis
notes: The edition title continues with source, editor, introduction/glossary, and preliminary-essay responsibility statements; those are modeled as responsibilities rather than folded into the preferred title.

agents.name: Named and anonymous guild, civic, royal, clerical, and administrative documentary creators
notes: A cataloging collective for the collection's many issuers, guild officers, scribes, officials, and institutions; it is not a claim of shared authorship, common date, or common physical witness.

agents.name: Joshua Toulmin Smith
notes: 1816–1869. Compiler and principal editor; Parts I and II were substantially complete at his death, while Part III remained to be collated and annotated.

agents.name: Lucy Toulmin Smith
notes: 1838–1911. Completed the edition, wrote the introduction, compiled the glossary, and supplied most unfinished collation, notes, and marginal abstracts.

agents.name: Lujo Brentano
notes: 1844–1931. Author of the separately scoped preliminary essay on guild history and trade unions, completed in January 1870.

agents.name: Richard Morris
notes: 1833–1894. Author of Appendix II and adviser to Lucy Toulmin Smith on the glossary.

agents.name: G. Parker
notes: The edition identifies him only as Mr G. Parker of Oxford; he supplied and twice collated the copy used for Appendix I.

agents.name: Robert Ricart
notes: Bristol town clerk who began the Kalendar in 1479 and was responsible for the Office of the Mayor section excerpted in Part III; later civic officers continued the larger manuscript.

form_statements.description: A prose edited documentary collection with prose editorial matter, essay, notes, glossary, and index. The XML's 72 L elements represent embedded quotations or locally lineated material, not a verse-form collection.

subject_statements.label: History and origins of trade unions
notes: This is Brentano's nineteenth-century historical argument, not a subject imposed on every medieval record.

summaries.value: English Gilds assembles selected returns, ordinances, customaries, charters, reports, and municipal records to document the organization and social practices of English religious, craft, and civic guilds. Part I prints forty-nine English returns from the 1388–89 royal inquiry, chiefly from London and Norfolk. Part II broadens the evidence through translated and English materials from towns across England. Part III presents four separate civic texts: Winchester's Olde Usages, Worcester ordinances, Robert Ricart's account of the Bristol mayor's office, and the Tettenhall customary. A fuller Norwich St George ordinance set and Richard Morris's grammatical notes form the two appendices. Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction frames the documentary project, while Lujo Brentano's independent five-part essay argues a history from early guilds to trade unions. The complete 1870 edition also contains Lucy's glossarial index and a general index, both absent from the repository XML.

The 1870 volume comprises Lucy Toulmin Smith's introduction (pp. xi–xlviii); Lujo Brentano's preliminary essay (pp. xlix–cxcix); Part I, forty-nine English guild returns plus an editorial introduction (pp. 1–123); Part II, eighteen town or source groupings plus an editorial introduction (pp. 125–346); Part III, an editorial introduction and four civic records (pp. 347–442); Appendix I, Norwich St George ordinances (pp. 443–460); Appendix II, Richard Morris's grammatical notes (pp. 461–462); a glossarial index (pp. 463–478); and a general index (pp. 479–483). The XML encodes eight DIV1 units—contents, introduction, essay, Parts I–III, and Appendices I–II—but does not encode the last two printed components.

The edition's claim to contain ordinances of more than one hundred guilds is a title and marketing boundary, not a machine-countable number of independent works or witnesses. Part I has forty-nine numbered returns; Part II groups many documents under eighteen place headings; Part III has four separate source texts; Appendix I adds a fuller Norwich witness. Editorial introductions, translations, notes, parallel texts, glossarial entries, and index entries must not be counted as equivalent documentary works.

extent.description: The complete edition has nine high-level intellectual components: introduction, preliminary essay, three documentary parts, two appendices, glossarial index, and general index. The repository XML has eight DIV1 containers because it also encodes a contents division but omits the glossary and index; within those are 87 DIV2 and 97 DIV3 structures. Part I contains 49 numbered returns plus an editor's introduction, Part II contains 18 place/source groupings plus an editor's introduction, and Part III contains four records plus an editor's introduction. These edition and encoding structures are not a stable count of independent works or physical manuscripts. The 72 L elements are embedded or quoted lineation within an overwhelmingly prose compilation.

content_parts.label: Introduction by Lucy Toulmin Smith
content_parts.summary: Explains the edition's completion after Joshua Toulmin Smith's death, its selected documentary purpose, the editors' responsibilities, and the social character of English guilds.

content_parts.label: On the History and Development of Gilds, and the Origin of Trade-Unions
content_parts.summary: Lujo Brentano's independent five-part historical essay on guild origins, religious guilds, guild merchants, craft guilds, and trade unions.

content_parts.label: Part I: Returns, in English
content_parts.summary: Forty-nine numbered English-language guild returns from the January 1389 inquiry, chiefly concerning London, Norwich, Lynn, Wiggenhall, East Winch, and Oxborough, preceded by an editor's introduction.
notes: The forty-nine printed returns are editorial units, not necessarily forty-nine independent physical manuscripts under current archival arrangement.

content_parts.label: Part II: Illustrations of the Ordinances and Usages of Early English Gilds
content_parts.summary: A heterogeneous selection of writs, returns, translations, ordinances, charters, reports, oaths, inventories, and other guild records arranged under eighteen place or source groupings.
notes: The nineteen DIV2 structures comprise one editor's introduction and eighteen top-level place/source groupings; many groupings contain multiple distinct documents, so no false item total is supplied.

content_parts.label: Part III: Gilds and Municipal Bodies
content_parts.summary: An editorial introduction followed by four separately sourced civic texts from Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, and Tettenhall.
notes: The four documentary items are counted in the child parts below; the parent item_count is zero to avoid double counting.

content_parts.label: The Olde Usages of the City of Winchester
content_parts.summary: Middle English civic usages transcribed from a damaged fourteenth-century two-skin roll, with editorial note and grammatical commentary.

content_parts.label: The Ordinances of Worcester
content_parts.summary: Selected Worcester civic ordinances, principally the eighty-two ordinances of 1467, with a calendar, later material, and editorial note.

content_parts.label: The Office of the Mayor of Bristol
content_parts.summary: The fourth part of Robert Ricart's Kalendar, initially set from Charles Innes Pocock's manuscript copy and later collated by Lucy Toulmin Smith against Bristol's original CC/2/7.
notes: The edition numbers paragraphs and omits a few manuscript side notes.

content_parts.label: The Costomary of the Manor of Tettenhall Regis
content_parts.summary: A customary based on an incomplete copy dated 22 July 1604 and filled or corrected from a later paper copy supplied by Mr Neve.

content_parts.label: Appendix I: Gild of St George, Norwich
content_parts.summary: A fuller set of Norwich St George ordinances taken through G. Parker's working copy and two collations of the Bodleian manuscript historically designated Rawlinson Misc. 1370.

content_parts.label: Appendix II: Grammatical Notes
content_parts.summary: Richard Morris's notes on the language of the East Midland guilds and the Winchester roll.

content_parts.label: Glossarial Index
content_parts.summary: Lucy Toulmin Smith's glossary of vocabulary in the edited texts, compiled with advice from Richard Morris.
notes: This component belongs to the complete edition but is absent from the repository XML.

content_parts.label: Index
content_parts.summary: The edition's general subject and reference index.
notes: This component belongs to the complete edition but is absent from the repository XML.

related_works.title: French version of the Winchester Usages formerly at Winchester College
notes: Lucy Toulmin Smith learned of this parallel version only after the English text was printed; it is collateral transmission evidence, not the source of the edited English text.

related_works.title: The Maire of Bristowe Is Kalendar
notes: Lucy Toulmin Smith's fuller Camden Society edition appeared in 1872; English Gilds prints only the Office of the Mayor portion.

evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for identifiers, title, responsibilities, 1963 source statement, retained 1892 additions, documentary content, languages, places, headings, notes, and the represented boundary through Appendix II.

evidence.citation: Computational inspection of EGilds.xml and the cached 1870 scan-data inventory.
evidence.locator: Element counts; part-level DIV/P/L/PB counts; FRONT/BODY/BACK inventory; scan-data leaves 668–691
evidence.summary: Confirms 8 DIV1, 87 DIV2, 97 DIV3, 1,532 P, 72 L, 658 PB, 916 ITEM, 1,127 NOTE1, and 871 FOREIGN elements; establishes part ranges and proves that the XML lacks the printed glossarial index and general index.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for the complete 1870 English Gilds scan.
evidence.summary: Direct book evidence for title, imprint, complete contents, pagination, responsibilities, documentary headings, glossary, and index.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1870 introduction.
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for editorial responsibilities, the state of the work at Joshua Toulmin Smith's death, the selection boundary, and Lucy's glossary and completion work.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for source and editorial notes in the 1870 edition.
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for Part I selection and punctuation policy and the distinct Winchester, Worcester, Bristol, Tettenhall, and Rawlinson source paths.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Lucy Toulmin Smith's August 1892 correction notice.
evidence.summary: Supports the 1892 revision date, obsolete bundle-reference warning, limited added notes, and missing reference-update leaf question.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for the UC Berkeley Law Library 1892 catalog record.
evidence.summary: Confirms a distinct 1892 N. Trübner plate reprint with added notes, cxcix plus 483 pages, OCLC 13747087.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for the current University of Michigan EGilds item.
evidence.summary: Confirms stable item identity, current institutional access, and current public-domain statement.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Jones's study of the 1388–89 inquiry.
evidence.summary: Reliable scholarship for the inquiry context and modern C 47/38–46 archival grouping without treating it as one manuscript.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Bristol Archives CC/2/7 and the University of Bristol Ricart description.
evidence.summary: Current institutional evidence for Robert Ricart, the Kalendar's exact modern shelfmark, Bristol custody, and the source scope of The Office of the Mayor.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for the Worcester City Collection.
evidence.summary: Current institutional evidence for Worcester City Archive custody and catalog access, while leaving the exact ordinance-volume shelfmark unresolved.

open_questions.question: What are the exact creators, dates, languages, documentary forms, and current source references for every selected document in Parts I and II?
next_steps: Create an item-level inventory from every printed heading and source note.
Crosswalk obsolete Public Record Office bundle numbers to current TNA C 47 references.
Record issuer, author, scribe, copyist, translator, date, language, and material witness separately for each document.

open_questions.question: Did the exact 1963 source copy omit the 1892 reference-update leaf and the glossary/index, or were those components omitted only in the U-M encoding?
next_steps: Inspect the University of Michigan 1963 volume's complete front and back matter.
Compare its pp. 348 and 431 with the 1870 scan and XML.
Revise the encoding boundary only after carrier-level evidence is available.

open_questions.question: What current shelfmarks identify the Winchester roll, Worcester ordinance volume, Tettenhall copies, and Rawlinson Misc. 1370?
next_steps: Request current crosswalks from the likely custodial repositories using the edition's physical descriptions.
Do not propagate the 1870 designations as current identifiers without repository confirmation.
Update part-to-witness bindings when exact records are verified.

open_questions.question: What editorial counting rule produced the title's claim of ordinances from more than one hundred guilds?
next_steps: Count unique guilds and documentary units in the complete 1870 contents and text, distinguishing multiple records for one guild and general civic texts.
Compare that count with Joshua Toulmin Smith's calendar of the full source corpus.
Retain the title phrase without turning it into an extent value until the rule is reconstructed.

notes: The cataloging subject is an edited selection and modern scholarly composite, not one medieval authored work.
The many historical records, printed editions, physical witnesses, digital encoding, repository artifact, and scan remain separate lineage layers.

The U-M header's language list is not propagated uncritically: Middle English is added from direct textual evidence, while Old English, German, and Greek are scoped as quotation rather than primary language.
The 72 XML L elements do not justify assigning verse form to this predominantly prose collection.
The glossarial index and general index are included in the complete edition boundary and content summary but explicitly marked absent from the repository XML.
No single current shelfmark is assigned to the Part II source set; its archival heterogeneity remains an item-level research problem.
